ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has entries for cooked navy beans and generic canned legumes; the brand-specific Heinz formulation with sweetened tomato sauce is not individually represented. Closest parent used: baked bean (canned).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers canned baked beans generically; Heinz brand-specific sweetened tomato sauce variant is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for canned baked beans as a food product; SNOMED CT food substance coverage for this category is limited.

serving.description: 1 small can (200g) or approximately half a 420g Heinz can
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one small 200g can of Heinz Baked Beans in Tomato Sauce, equivalent to approximately half of a standard 420g retail can.

nutrient_source.title: Heinz Baked Beans in Tomato Sauce — Nutrition Information Panel, Singapore product label (420g can); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for baked beans, canned, with tomato sauce (FDC ID 173744)
nutrient_source.organization: H.J. Heinz Company / Kraft Heinz (Singapore NIP); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values taken from the Heinz Singapore product NIP (420g can), which provides per-100g and per-serving data. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for canned baked beans in tomato sauce used for cross-validation of micronutrient values (iron, folate, potassium, magnesium, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, vitamins). Values are consistent between sources within expected product variation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Canned Baked Beans in Tomato Sauce — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Research Service, Sydney)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~40 (low) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for canned baked beans in tomato sauce. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 28.0g per 200g serving) / 100 = 11.2. Available carbohydrate per 200g serving = 14.0g/100g × 2 = 28.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 200mg/100g (400mg per 200g serving = 20% of 2,000mg/day limit); partially offset by potassium 250mg/100g (500mg/serving)
hypertension.guidance: Heinz Baked Beans contribute a moderate sodium load of 400mg per standard 200g serving; individuals with hypertension should account for this within their daily sodium budget of 2,000mg and consider pairing with low-sodium foods, noting that the 500mg potassium per serving provides a partial counterbalancing effect on blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40), GL ~11.2 per 200g serving; 7.6g dietary fibre per serving from resistant starch and soluble pectin in tomato sauce blunts postprandial glucose rise; moderate sugar 9.2g/serving from sweetened tomato sauce warrants awareness
type2_diabetes.guidance: Heinz Baked Beans are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their low glycemic index (~40) and high dietary fibre (7.6g per 200g serving), which significantly attenuates postprandial glucose absorption; however, the 9.2g of sugar per serving from the sweetened tomato sauce should be factored into carbohydrate counting, and the standard (not extra-sweetened) variety should be chosen.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol; high dietary fibre (3.8g/100g) supports LDL reduction; moderate sodium (200mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular caution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Heinz Baked Beans are a heart-friendly canned food with negligible saturated fat (0.2g/serving), zero cholesterol, and 7.6g dietary fibre per 200g serving that supports LDL cholesterol reduction; the moderate sodium content (400mg/serving) should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension or heart failure, and the food can be incorporated into a cardioprotective diet as a plant-protein and fibre source.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory legume base; folate (25mcg/100g) supports immune regulation; lycopene from tomato sauce provides antioxidant activity; iron (4.2mg/100g) benefits RA patients with anaemia of chronic disease
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Navy beans in Heinz Baked Beans provide anti-inflammatory dietary fibre supporting microbiome diversity, folate supporting methylation and immune regulation, and the tomato sauce contributes lycopene with antioxidant activity; the high iron content (8.4mg per 200g serving, approximately 52% of men's RDI) is particularly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients with anaemia of chronic disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Heinz Baked Beans in Tomato Sauce is a globally recognised canned convenience food produced by H.J. Heinz Company (now Kraft Heinz), first manufactured in 1901. The product consists of navy beans (haricot beans, Phaseolus vulgaris) slow-cooked and packed in a sweetened tomato sauce. In Singapore, the iconic orange can is a pantry staple found in supermarkets islandwide and is consumed as part of a Singaporean-style Western breakfast alongside fried eggs, toast, and sausages, or as a standalone meal with bread or rice. The texture is soft and creamy with a mildly sweet, tangy tomato flavour. Nutritionally, Heinz Baked Beans are one of the most clinically valuable canned convenience foods available: low glycemic index (~40), 7.6g dietary fibre per 200g serving (approximately 27% of the 28g/day RDI), 8g plant protein per serving, and an exceptional iron content of 8.4mg per 200g serving (approximately 52% of the adult male RDI). The resistant starch in navy beans combined with soluble pectin from the tomato sauce creates a viscous digestive environment that significantly blunts postprandial glucose absorption, making this food particularly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The primary nutritional cautions are moderate sodium (400mg per 200g serving, 20% of the 2,000mg/day limit) and a surprising sugar content of 9.2g per 200g serving from the sweetened tomato sauce formulation. Heinz has progressively reduced sodium in its Singapore formulation over successive reformulations. The product is vegetarian, vegan, halal-certified, and dairy-free, making it suitable for diverse dietary requirements across Singapore's multicultural population.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from the Heinz Singapore product Nutrition Information Panel (NIP), which is a manufacturer-verified label source. Micronutrient values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for baked beans, canned, with tomato sauce (FDC ID 173744). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All values are internally consistent and supported by approved sources.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per the Heinz Singapore product NIP. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — this is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per product NIP and consistent with USDA data for canned legumes. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported on the Heinz Singapore NIP and is not available in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this specific product at the level of individual fatty acid fractions. water_g is set to -1: moisture content is not reported on the Heinz Singapore NIP; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 73g water per 100g for canned baked beans in tomato sauce but this value was not carried forward as it applies to a generic product rather than the Heinz-specific formulation. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as (GI 40 × available carbohydrate 28.0g) / 100 = 11.2. The food_subgroup description retains the input note that 1 standard serving = 200g. The cultural region_tags in the input contained narrative text rather than concise region codes; this has been normalised in the output. Chinese traditional and simplified forms are identical for this product name.

improvement_actions_taken: Added mandatory Hindi local name and alias (हेंज़ बेक्ड बीन्स टमाटर सॉस में) to satisfy G6 Singaporean multilingual requirement.
Restructured health_context to use required keys (hypertension, type2_diabetes, cardiovascular_disease) with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model Q7 requirement.
Added cardiovascular_disease health context entry (not present in input) to satisfy Q7.
Populated aliases_by_language structured object and verified consistency with flat aliases array per Q8 and aliases consistency rule.

Documented all -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) and confirmed-zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) in notes per G3 and G4 rules.
Added FoodEx2 code A00LC with closest_parent match type and gap note to satisfy Q5 ontological alignment.
Expanded natural_language_description to exceed 200-character minimum with clinical, cultural, and nutritional detail per Q6.
Normalised nutrient field names to RESULT template conventions (e.g., energy_kcal, protein_g, carbohydrate_g, dietary_fibre_g, sodium_mg).
Added nutrient_source block with dual-source citation (Heinz Singapore NIP + USDA FDC SR Legacy cross-validation).
Verified GL calculation: (40 × 28.0g available carbohydrate per 200g serving) / 100 = 11.2.
Corrected Q8 failure by ensuring all entries from aliases_by_language (including romanized forms) are explicitly present in the flat 'aliases' array.
